1: YHWH opens the closing chapter with a sovereignty claim that destabilizes any human attempt to contain him: Heaven is my throne and the earth is my footstool; where is the house you would build for me, and where is my resting place? The throne-footstool image is the standard iconography of ancient Near Eastern kingship — but YHWH's throne is the cosmos itself. The rhetorical question challenges the temple theology that assumes YHWH needs or is contained by a building. Stephen quotes this verse in his climactic speech (Acts 7:48-50) as the reason Israel's leaders, who killed the prophets and now Jesus, have fundamentally misunderstood YHWH's nature.
2: All these things my hand has made, and all these things exist, declares YHWH (YHWH, disp 4). But this is the one I will regard: the humble and contrite in spirit (ruach, disp 4), who trembles at my word. The reversal: YHWH who fills the cosmos looks not for grand structures but for a crushed and humble ruach. The trembling-at-YHWH's-word is the mark of the remnant throughout Isaiah (cf. 57:15; 66:5). The ruach-contrast: YHWH's ruach fills the cosmos; the humble person's ruach is what YHWH inhabits. Ps 51:17 makes the same claim: a broken and contrite heart YHWH will not despise.
3: Whoever slaughters an ox is like one who kills a man; whoever sacrifices a lamb is like one who breaks a dog's neck — the shocking equation of orthodox sacrifice with violence. The verse is not condemning all sacrifice but the sacrifice of those whose heart-condition is that of v 3b: who have chosen their own ways and whose nefesh (soul, disp 3) delights in their abominations. Sacrifice without heart-submission is no different from murder and ritual impurity. The heart-posture of v 2 (humble, contrite) is the necessary condition for any worship to be received.
4: I in turn will choose what they dread and bring their fears upon them, because when I called no one answered; when I spoke they did not listen. The answering-calling pattern again: YHWH called (65:1-2, 12), they did not answer — therefore YHWH sends them what they most feared. The judgment is judicial and proportional: their rejection of YHWH's call results in experiencing the fears they generated for themselves by their choices.
5: Hear the word of YHWH (YHWH, disp 4), you who tremble at his word: Your brothers who hate you and reject you for my name's sake have said, Let YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) be glorified, that we may see your joy — but they shall be put to shame. The community of those who tremble at YHWH's word (v 2) is being persecuted by those who mock their trust in YHWH — saying sarcastically that they are waiting to see if YHWH will vindicate them. The answer: the mockers will be put to shame, and the trembling community will see the city's joy (v 10).
6: Listen — an uproar from the city, a voice from the temple, the voice of YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) repaying his enemies what they deserve. The voice of YHWH from the temple is the covenant-judgment sound (Ps 29; Joel 3:16; Amos 1:2). The repaying of enemies is the reversal of the exile's humiliation and the answer to the prayer of 63:15-64:12. The temple, once destroyed (64:11), is now the source of YHWH's victorious voice.
7: Before she went into labor she gave birth; before her pain came on her, she delivered a son. The instantaneous birth — without labor — reverses the normal order. The birth of the new community is YHWH's miraculous act: no human effort, no natural process, no suffering required on Zion's part. The son born in an instant is the community of the new creation, born in the moment of divine decision.
8: Who has heard of such a thing? Who has seen things like these? Can a land be delivered in a single day? Can a nation be born in an instant? For as soon as Zion was in labor, she gave birth to her children. The rhetorical impossibility-questions frame the event as miracle. The single-day nation-birth is the maximum form of YHWH's creative sovereignty: what took generations in the exodus-settlement takes one day in the new exodus.
9: Would I bring to the point of birth and not cause delivery? says YHWH (YHWH, disp 4); Would I, who give birth, then close the womb? says your God (elohim, disp 4). The divine rhetorical question uses the midwife/mother image: YHWH has begun the process (brought to birth) and will complete it (not close the womb). The promise is grounded in the logic of YHWH's own purposes: he does not begin what he does not finish. This is the same assurance as 55:11 (my word will not return to me empty).
10: Rejoice with Jerusalem and be glad for her, all you who love her; rejoice with her in her joy, all you who mourn over her. The call to communal rejoicing extends to all who have mourned for Jerusalem — including those who mourn in exile. The mourning-turned-joy pattern is the same as 61:3 (garment of praise instead of faint spirit). The community of mourners becomes the community of rejoicing by YHWH's act alone.
11: So that you may nurse and be satisfied at her comforting breasts; so that you may drink deeply and delight in her abundant glory (kabod, disp 2). The nursing-Jerusalem image: the city herself nourishes her children. The kabod (glory) of Jerusalem is itself nourishing — the community draws life from YHWH's glory-presence in the city. The abundance (zizon) of her glory suggests overflowing wealth that more than suffices.
12: For thus says YHWH (YHWH, disp 4): I will extend prosperity (shalom, disp 2) to her like a river, and the wealth of nations like a flooding stream; and you will nurse, you will be carried on her hip, and bounced on her knees. The shalom extended like a river: the comprehensive wellbeing flows without ceasing, like the river of the new Jerusalem (Rev 22:1). The nursing-carried-bounced images: the restored community is treated as an infant by YHWH and by Jerusalem — completely provided for, completely secure, completely delighted in.
13: As a mother comforts her child, so I will comfort you; and you will be comforted in Jerusalem. The comfort promised in 40:1 — comfort, comfort my people — reaches its fulfillment here. YHWH as mother-comforter is the culmination of the nursing/carrying images that began at 40:11 (shepherd carrying lambs). The comfort is located specifically in Jerusalem: the city and the divine presence are inseparable in the new creation.
14: When you see this, your heart will rejoice and your bones will flourish like the grass; the hand of YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) will be made known (yada, disp 2) to his servants, and his fury to his enemies. The physical flourishing (bones like grass) is the embodied form of the inner rejoicing — wholeness reaches the body. The knowing of YHWH's hand by the servants is the final form of the exodus-knowledge: YHWH revealed his character in the exodus; he reveals it again in the new creation's deliverance. The enemies experience the same divine power as fury rather than grace.
15: For see, YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) will come in fire, with his chariots like a whirlwind, to repay his anger in fury and his rebuke with flames of fire. The warrior-theophany closes the book as it opened the restoration-section (63:1-6). The fire-chariot-whirlwind is Elijah's pattern (2 Kgs 2:11) and Ezekiel's chariot-vision (Ezek 1). YHWH's coming for judgment is as real and physical as his coming for restoration.
16: For by fire and by his sword YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) will execute judgment (shaphat, disp 2) upon all flesh, and the slain of YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) shall be many. The judgment (shaphat) is comprehensive — all flesh — and lethal. The courtroom-judgment vocabulary (shaphat) is applied to cosmic warfare: YHWH both judges and executes. The many slain is the reverse of the many made righteous in 53:11-12.
17: Those who consecrate and purify themselves to enter the gardens, following the one in their midst, eating pig and abominable things and mice — they shall come to an end together, declares YHWH (YHWH, disp 4). The garden-cult practitioners of 65:3-4 reappear here for their final judgment. Their ritual self-purification for abominable worship is the ultimate religious inversion — the language of qodesh used for what is tame (impure). YHWH's qodesh/holy (disp 2) designation is reserved for those who tremble at his word (v 2, 5), not for those who consecrate themselves to idolatry.
18: For I know their works and their thoughts, and the time is coming to gather all nations and peoples of every language; they will come and see my glory (kabod, disp 2). The gathering of all nations to see YHWH's kabod is the universal scope of the whole book's vision: from the nations gathering against Zion (chapters 1-12) to the nations streaming to Zion's light (chapters 40-66). The kabod they will see is the same glory that filled the temple (6:3) — YHWH's sovereign, holy beauty displayed to all.
19: I will set a sign among them, and from them I will send survivors to the nations — to Tarshish, Pul, Lud who draw the bow, Tubal and Javan, to the far coastlands that have not heard my fame or seen my glory (kabod, disp 2). The survivors are sent as missionaries: those who saw the kabod go out to declare it to those who have not. The sending-pattern is the Isaianic servant-pattern applied now to the whole restored community (49:6: a light to the nations). The listing of specific distant nations emphasizes the genuinely universal scope.
20: They shall bring all your brothers from all the nations as an offering to YHWH (YHWH, disp 4) — riding on horses, in chariots and litters, on mules and camels — to my holy (qodesh, disp 2) mountain Jerusalem, says YHWH (YHWH, disp 4), as the people of Israel bring their grain offering in clean vessels to the house of YHWH (YHWH, disp 4). The exiled brothers are brought back as an offering — the community's return to the holy mountain is itself a liturgical act. The nations who bring them are functioning as priests of the offering. The qodesh/holy mountain is the destination toward which the whole book has been moving.
21: And from among them I will also take some as priests and Levites, says YHWH (YHWH, disp 4). From among the gathered nations YHWH takes priests and Levites — the most radical universalism: the priestly office, reserved for one tribe, is opened to those outside Israel entirely. This fulfills 61:6 (you will be called priests of YHWH) applied now to the previously excluded nations. Rev 1:6 and 5:10 apply this promise to the church — made priests to serve YHWH.
22: For as the new heavens and the new earth that I am making shall endure before me, declares YHWH (YHWH, disp 4), so shall your offspring and your name endure. The durability of the new creation guarantees the durability of the community: the same olam-permanence of the cosmos is the olam-permanence of the covenant people. Name and offspring — identity and continuity — are as permanent as the new heavens and earth.
23: From one new moon to the next, and from one Sabbath to the next, all people shall come and worship before me, declares YHWH (YHWH, disp 4). The liturgical calendar (new moon, Sabbath) continues in the new creation, but now all flesh participates — the calendar that structured Israel's covenant time becomes the calendar of universal worship. The weekly and monthly rhythms of creation (Gen 1:14) become the rhythms of eschatological worship: creation and new creation are unified in the praise of YHWH.
24: And they shall go out and look at the corpses of those who rebelled against me, for their worm shall not die, and their fire shall not be quenched, and they shall be an abhorrence to all flesh. The book closes with the most severe image in the entire corpus: the permanent judgment of the rebels, visible as a perpetual warning from the new Jerusalem. The undying worm and unquenched fire are quoted by Jesus in Mark 9:44-48 as the description of Gehenna. The final word of Isaiah is not comfort but judgment — the book does not end with the new creation alone but with the contrast between those who worship YHWH in the new creation and those whose rebellion made them an abhorrence. The whole book's trajectory — YHWH's holiness judging and redeeming — ends with both dimensions fully in view.
